excluded_note: 4 of the 23 corners aren't on this ribbon yet — their street history didn't clear our two-independent-sources rule, so we left them off rather than guess. Absence here means "not yet sourced," not "nothing happened."

beats.title: Phan Văn Trị
beats.fact: The street honors the poet Phan Văn Trị (1830–1910), whose satirical verse answered the early colonial collaborators.

beats.title: Lăng Ông
beats.fact: Lê Văn Duyệt (1763–1831) served as the Nguyễn dynasty's viceroy of Gia Định province (southern Vietnam) under both Emperor Gia Long and Emperor Minh Mạng. His tomb in Bình Thạnh was constructed after his death in 1831 and is known formally as 'Lăng Ông Bà Chiểu' — the Marshal's Tomb in Bà Chiểu. The complex was renovated in 1937 and again in 2008.

beats.title: Bình Lợi
beats.fact: The Bình Lợi Railway Bridge was completed in 1902 by French contractor Société de Construction de Levallois-Perret (formerly Compagnie des Établissements Eiffel, associated with Gustave Eiffel). It measured 276 m across six spans with a 90-degree rotating span on the Thủ Đức side for river traffic, and was the first bridge to cross the Saigon River, forming part of the Saigon–Nha Trang railway line.

beats.title: Ung Văn Khiêm
beats.fact: The street honors Ung Văn Khiêm (1910–1991), a revolutionary who served as North Vietnam's foreign minister.

beats.title: Ung Văn Khiêm (tây)
beats.fact: The street honors Ung Văn Khiêm (1910–1991), a revolutionary who served as North Vietnam's foreign minister.

beats.title: Nơ Trang Long
beats.fact: N'Trang Lơng (Pa-Trang-Loeng), born 1870 in Bu Par village, was a Mnong chief who led the largest Indochinese tribal uprising against French colonial rule. The resistance lasted from 1912 until his death in 1935. The street in Bình Thạnh was named in his honour.

beats.title: Chợ Bà Chiểu
beats.fact: Chợ Bà Chiểu began as an informal 'chợ xóm' (neighbourhood morning market) before being formally constructed in 1942 on an 8,500 sq-m site. A renovation in 1987 added a second floor and modern facilities. Today the market covers about 8,400 sq m with nearly 800 stalls and is classified as one of HCMC's five top-tier public markets.

beats.title: Hàng Xanh
beats.fact: The name 'Ngã Tư Hàng Xanh' (Green-Row Crossroads) dates to around 1945, when the junction was planted with rows of sanh trees (a shade tree related to the banyan). 'Hàng xanh' literally means 'green row' and refers to those shade trees.

beats.title: Điện Biên Phủ
beats.fact: Điện Biên Phủ Street in Ho Chi Minh City is named after the Battle of Điện Biên Phủ (13 March–7 May 1954), the decisive French defeat by Việt Minh forces that ended the First Indochina War and led to the 1954 Geneva Accords. Cities across Vietnam named streets after the battle as a commemoration of the victory.

beats.title: Hàng Xanh
beats.fact: The Hanoi Highway (formerly Biên Hòa Highway) was constructed between July 1957 and April 1961, starting at Phan Thanh Giản Bridge; it passes through the Hàng Xanh intersection and confirmed the junction as a major traffic node.

beats.title: Cầu Sài Gòn
beats.fact: Construction of Cầu Sài Gòn (then called Newport Bridge) began in 1959; it was formally opened on 28 June 1961. Designed by Drake and Piper Johnson, the bridge spans 1,010 metres over the Saigon River connecting Bình Thạnh to what was then District 2.

beats.title: Thanh Đa
beats.fact: Thanh Đa Residential Estate (Cư Xá Thanh Đa) was built in the 1960s on the Thanh Đa peninsula — an oxbow loop of the Saigon River covering wards 27 and 28, Bình Thạnh — as a housing complex for military officers of the South Vietnamese government. The estate has been pending redevelopment for decades and remains largely intact, giving the peninsula an unusual time-capsule quality.

beats.title: Tân Cảng
beats.fact: Newport (Tân Cảng) was built between 1965 and 1967 as a US Army and ARVN logistics base at a cost exceeding $50 million. Four deep-water berths opened by July 1967; it was MACV's highest-priority construction project. The 71st Transport Battalion operated it from 1966 to 1975.

beats.title: Văn Thánh
beats.fact: Văn Thánh Tourist Village (Làng du lịch Văn Thánh) was developed as a Saigontourist-managed leisure complex on the Thị Nghè riverbank in Bình Thạnh, covering about 5.5 ha of land and 2–5 ha of water. It is also the name-sake of the adjacent Văn Thánh Park metro station on Metro Line 1.

beats.title: Cầu Sài Gòn
beats.fact: With the ARVN commander receiving President Dương Văn Minh's capitulation order at 10:24, the estimated 4,000 lbs of demolition charges rigged on the bridge were never detonated. At 10:30 AM a PAVN column crossed the bridge, marking the effective end of the war.

beats.title: Thị Nghè
beats.fact: A World Bank-funded restoration project beginning in 2002 included sewer installation, dredging, and relocation of canal-side slums. By 2013–2015 three bridges — including Lê Văn Sỹ, Kiệu, and Bông — were rebuilt; tourist boat tours launched along the restored Nhiêu Lộc–Thị Nghè canal in September 2015.

beats.title: Cầu Bông
beats.fact: Bông Bridge was among three canal bridges rebuilt as part of the World Bank-funded Nhiêu Lộc–Thị Nghè canal restoration project, which also included Lê Văn Sỹ and Kiệu bridges.

beats.title: Cầu Kiệu
beats.fact: Cầu Kiệu was rebuilt as part of the World Bank-funded Nhiêu Lộc–Thị Nghè canal restoration project, which also rebuilt Lê Văn Sỹ and Bông bridges. The restoration cleaned and widened the canal, installed sewer infrastructure, relocated canal-side residents, and created a greened waterway promenade.

beats.title: Tân Cảng
beats.fact: After 1975 the base was repurposed by the state; by 2014 Vingroup broke ground on Vinhomes Central Park on the site. Landmark 81 (461.2 m, 81 floors, designed by Atkins) was completed in 2018, becoming Vietnam's tallest building.

beats.title: Tân Cảng (nam)
beats.fact: Vinhomes Central Park was built on the former Newport (Tân Cảng) military port site. The project address is 208 Nguyễn Hữu Cảnh Street, Ward 22, Bình Thạnh, with the southern sector fronting the Saigon River. Metro Line 1 (Bến Thành–Suối Tiên) serves the complex via Tân Cảng Station (Station 5), placed directly adjacent to Vinhomes Central Park.

beats.title: Tân Cảng (nam)
beats.fact: Landmark 81 (461.2 m, 81 floors, designed by Atkins) was completed in 2018 as Vietnam's tallest building, rising above the Vinhomes Central Park complex. The SkyView observatory on floors 79–81 is the city's highest public viewpoint.

beats.title: Bình Lợi
beats.fact: A new road bridge opened in 2019 to replace the deteriorated railway structure. The Ministry of Transport agreed to preserve part of the old Bình Lợi Railway Bridge — one of the colonial-era spans with its riveted steel arch — as a heritage relic alongside the new crossing.

beats.title: Đinh Tiên Hoàng
beats.fact: The Ho Chi Minh City People's Council approved the renaming of Đinh Tiên Hoàng Street in Bình Thạnh to Lê Văn Duyệt Street on 11 July 2020; the street signs were officially changed on 16 September 2020. The segment from Cầu Bông to Phan Đăng Lưu had previously borne the name Lê Văn Duyệt from 1955 to 1975, making the 2020 change a restoration of the earlier name.

beats.title: Đinh Tiên Hoàng
beats.fact: The renaming was driven by the location of the national-level historical site Lăng Ông Bà Chiểu (Tomb of Lê Văn Duyệt) on the street; local residents supported restoring the original name to match the district's most significant heritage monument.

beats.title: Văn Thánh
beats.fact: The park is a privately managed entertainment complex with lake pavilions, restaurants, craft-village replicas (stone-carving, weaving, pottery), and a green lawn frequented by pensioners doing morning tai chi and school groups.

beats.title: Thị Nghè
beats.fact: Nguyễn Thị Khánh, daughter of mandarin Nguyễn Cửu Vân, built a bridge across the waterway here to ease travel. Her husband held the rank 'Nghè' (equivalent to a doctoral title), so the bridge became 'Cầu Bà Nghè' and the waterway 'Sông Bà Nghè.' Over time this shortened to 'Thị Nghè.'

beats.title: Cầu Bông
beats.fact: Scholar Trương Vĩnh Ký's 1882 publication 'Ancient Gia Định Bay Landscape' records four early bridges on the Thị Nghè canal including Bông Bridge (Cầu Bông) — confirming it as one of Saigon's oldest documented canal crossings.

beats.title: Cầu Kiệu
beats.fact: Kiệu Bridge (Cầu Kiệu) is one of Ho Chi Minh City's oldest bridges, originally called 'Bridge No. 3' (the third crossing of the Thị Nghè canal) or 'Bridge of Xóm Kiệu' (Village of pickled Allium chinense). It connected Tân Định (District 1) with Xã Tài Market (later Phú Nhuận Market), sitting at the Phú Nhuận / District 1 boundary — not within Bình Thạnh.

beats.title: Nơ Trang Long
beats.fact: Nơ Trang Long street runs from near Bình Lợi Bridge to the Bình Hoà intersection, passing through multiple wards and intersecting Phan Văn Trị, Nguyễn Xí, and Lê Quang Định.

beats.title: Thanh Đa
beats.fact: Bình Quới Tourist Villages were established on the peninsula in the 1970s, mimicking traditional rural architecture with manicured gardens, open-air restaurants, and river views. The villages have operated continuously as a leisure destination, making the peninsula the city's closest approximation of rural Mekong scenery.

beats.title: Lăng Ông
beats.fact: On the 30th day of the 7th lunar month each year, the Lăng Ông Bà Chiểu festival draws Vietnamese and Chinese-Vietnamese pilgrims who honour Lê Văn Duyệt for his fair policies toward the Chinese community during his administration. The tomb is a nationally recognised historical site.

beats.title: Đa Kao
beats.fact: Đa Kao is a neighbourhood within the northern part of District 1 (now Tân Định ward), bordering Bình Thạnh across the Nhiêu Lộc–Thị Nghè canal. It is home to a concentration of French colonial-era villas, consulates, and heritage lanes. The neighbourhood's name is an older romanisation of a Vietnamese place name in this historically elite residential quarter of Saigon.

beats.title: Chợ Bà Chiểu
beats.fact: The name 'Bà Chiểu' has competing historical explanations. One theory links it to five markets named for the five wives of Military Commander Nguyễn Ngọc Thắng (1798–1866), a Nguyễn dynasty general who led early resistance against the French in southern Vietnam. Another suggests 'chiểu' derives from a local natural pond or a female deity worshipped near one.
